questions.question: A 55-year-old smoker presents with a persistent, non-scrapable white patch on the lateral tongue for 6 weeks. The most appropriate initial management is:
questions.option_b: Empirical antifungal therapy for 2 weeks only
questions.option_c: Eliminate risk factors and perform biopsy of persistent lesion
questions.option_d: Prescribe topical steroids and review in 1 month
questions.explanation: A persistent non‑scrapable white lesion on a high‑risk site (lateral tongue) warrants biopsy after removing obvious irritants and addressing risk factors.

questions.question: Which clinical feature most strongly suggests oral squamous cell carcinoma rather than a benign traumatic ulcer?
questions.option_a: Painful ulcer on buccal mucosa for 3 days
questions.option_b: Ulcer with indurated base persisting >2 weeks
questions.option_c: Ulcer healing after removal of sharp tooth
questions.option_d: Ulcer covered by yellow fibrin for 4 days
questions.explanation: Induration and persistence beyond ~2 weeks are red flags for malignancy.

questions.question: Erythroplakia is best described as:
questions.option_a: A white lesion that can be scraped off
questions.option_b: A red patch not clinically/pathologically diagnosable as another condition
questions.option_c: A red lesion always caused by Candida
questions.option_d: A red patch with no malignant potential
questions.explanation: Erythroplakia is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion and carries a high rate of severe dysplasia/carcinoma on biopsy.

questions.question: The most common malignant tumor of the oral cavity is:
questions.option_b: Adenoid cystic carcinoma
questions.explanation: Oral squamous cell carcinoma is by far the most common oral malignancy.

questions.question: A white lesion that wipes off leaving an erythematous surface is most consistent with:
questions.option_b: Lichen planus
questions.explanation: Pseudomembranous candidiasis forms removable plaques; scraping reveals erythematous mucosa.

questions.question: Which is most characteristic of oral lichen planus?
questions.option_b: Bilateral interlacing white striae (Wickham striae) on buccal mucosa
questions.option_c: Solitary vesicle on hard palate only
questions.explanation: Reticular oral lichen planus typically presents with bilateral Wickham striae on the buccal mucosa.

questions.question: In mucous membrane pemphigoid, blister formation occurs primarily:
questions.option_b: Subepithelial at the basement membrane zone
questions.explanation: Mucous membrane pemphigoid is a subepithelial blistering disorder at the basement membrane zone.

questions.question: A positive Nikolsky sign is classically associated with:
questions.option_b: Pleomorphic adenoma
questions.explanation: Pemphigus vulgaris causes fragile intraepithelial bullae and a positive Nikolsky sign.

questions.question: A salivary gland malignancy strongly associated with perineural invasion and pain is:
questions.option_b: Pleomorphic adenoma
questions.explanation: Adenoid cystic carcinoma commonly shows perineural invasion, causing pain/paresthesia and local recurrence.

questions.question: A well-circumscribed radiolucency at the apex of a non-vital tooth most likely represents:
questions.option_b: Periapical granuloma/radicular cyst spectrum
questions.explanation: A periapical radiolucency associated with a non‑vital tooth is most commonly an inflammatory periapical lesion.

questions.question: Which oral lesion is most strongly associated with human papillomavirus (high-risk types) in the oropharynx?
questions.option_b: Squamous cell carcinoma of the tonsillar region
questions.explanation: HPV-associated SCC commonly involves the oropharynx (tonsil/base of tongue), often linked to high-risk HPV types.

questions.question: A non-healing extraction socket with exposed bone for >8 weeks in a patient taking antiresorptive therapy suggests:
questions.option_b: Medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ)
questions.explanation: Persistent exposed necrotic bone in the jaws in a patient on antiresorptives/antiangiogenics is consistent with MRONJ.

questions.question: The most common intraoral site for oral SCC is:
questions.option_b: Lower lip vermilion
questions.option_c: Floor of mouth and ventrolateral tongue
questions.explanation: High-risk sites include floor of mouth and ventrolateral tongue; lower lip SCC is common but is often considered lip cancer.

questions.question: A painless, firm, slowly enlarging mass on the posterior lateral hard palate is most likely a tumor of:
questions.option_b: Major submandibular gland
questions.explanation: Minor salivary gland tumors often present as palatal masses; pleomorphic adenoma is common.

questions.question: Warthin tumor is most classically associated with:
questions.option_b: Smoking and parotid gland
questions.explanation: Warthin tumor typically occurs in the parotid, often in older male smokers, and may be bilateral.

questions.question: Which salivary gland tumor is the most common benign tumor overall?
questions.option_b: Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
questions.explanation: Pleomorphic adenoma is the most common benign salivary gland tumor.

questions.question: The classic clinical presentation of a mucocele is:
questions.option_a: Painless, fluctuant bluish swelling of the lower lip
questions.option_b: Indurated ulcer on lateral tongue
questions.option_c: Bilateral white striae on buccal mucosa
questions.explanation: Mucoceles are mucous extravasation phenomena, most often on the lower lip after minor trauma.

questions.question: A ranula differs from a mucocele primarily because a ranula:
questions.option_b: Occurs on the floor of mouth, often from the sublingual gland
questions.explanation: Ranulas are mucous extravasation cysts in the floor of mouth, frequently from the sublingual gland.

questions.question: A patient has thick, non-scrapable white lesions on buccal mucosa along the occlusal plane, bilaterally, with a history of cheek biting. Most likely diagnosis:
questions.option_b: Linea alba/morsicatio
questions.explanation: Bilateral lesions along the occlusal plane with a habit suggest frictional/biting changes rather than true leukoplakia.

questions.question: Which feature favors traumatic ulcer over SCC?
questions.option_b: Persists >3 weeks
questions.option_c: Clear traumatic source and resolves within 10–14 days after removal
questions.explanation: Traumatic ulcers should improve after eliminating the cause; persistence/induration suggests malignancy.

questions.question: A 22-year-old has recurrent minor aphthae. The typical location is:
questions.option_b: Attached gingiva
questions.explanation: Recurrent aphthae occur mostly on non-keratinized mucosa (buccal/labial mucosa, ventral tongue, floor of mouth).

questions.question: Herpangina (Coxsackie) most commonly presents with:
questions.option_a: Vesicles and ulcers on posterior soft palate/tonsillar pillars with fever
questions.option_b: Non-scrapable white plaque on lateral tongue
questions.option_c: Indurated ulcer on floor of mouth
questions.explanation: Herpangina causes posterior oral vesicles/ulcers and systemic symptoms in children.

questions.question: Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis is most associated with:
questions.option_a: HSV-1 in children with diffuse painful gingivitis and multiple vesicles/ulcers
questions.option_b: HSV-2 confined to keratinized mucosa
questions.explanation: Primary HSV-1 infection in children causes widespread oral ulcers/vesicles with gingivitis and systemic symptoms.

questions.question: Recurrent intraoral herpes most commonly affects:
questions.option_b: Keratinized mucosa such as hard palate and attached gingiva
questions.explanation: Recurrent intraoral HSV tends to appear on keratinized mucosa (hard palate, attached gingiva).

questions.question: A unilateral painful vesicular eruption on palate in an older adult, respecting midline, suggests:
questions.option_b: Aphthous stomatitis
questions.explanation: Zoster is dermatomal and unilateral, often not crossing the midline.

questions.question: Kaposi sarcoma lesions in the mouth most often appear as:
questions.option_b: Red-purple macules/plaques/nodules, especially on palate/gingiva
questions.explanation: Kaposi sarcoma (HHV-8) presents as violaceous lesions, commonly in immunosuppressed patients.

questions.question: Which condition classically causes a 'cobblestone' appearance of oral mucosa?
questions.option_b: Iron deficiency anemia
questions.explanation: Crohn disease can produce mucosal tags, swelling, and a cobblestone appearance; oral granulomatosis may be seen.

questions.question: Which oral finding is most typical in iron deficiency anemia?
questions.option_b: Atrophic glossitis with burning and angular cheilitis
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency can cause atrophic glossitis, mucosal pallor, and angular cheilitis.

questions.question: A patient with long-standing diabetes reports burning mouth and has erythematous mucosa under a denture. Most likely diagnosis:
questions.option_b: Pemphigus vulgaris
questions.explanation: Denture stomatitis often involves Candida and appears erythematous under a denture, especially with diabetes/immunosuppression.

questions.question: Oral hairy leukoplakia is associated with:
questions.option_b: HPV-6 only
questions.explanation: Hairy leukoplakia is EBV-associated and classically seen in immunosuppressed states (e.g., HIV).

questions.question: The most common odontogenic cyst is:
questions.option_b: Dentigerous cyst
questions.explanation: Radicular cysts associated with non-vital teeth are the most common odontogenic cysts.

questions.question: A cyst that surrounds the crown of an unerupted tooth and attaches at the CEJ is:
questions.option_b: Radicular cyst
questions.explanation: Dentigerous cyst encloses the crown of an unerupted tooth with attachment at the CEJ.

questions.question: Which odontogenic lesion is notable for recurrence and association with PTCH mutations in Gorlin syndrome?
questions.option_b: Odontogenic keratocyst
questions.explanation: OKC (keratocystic odontogenic tumor in older terms) is recurrence-prone and associated with Gorlin syndrome.

questions.question: A multilocular 'soap-bubble' radiolucency in posterior mandible is most suggestive of:
questions.option_b: Radicular cyst
questions.explanation: Ameloblastoma commonly appears as a multilocular radiolucency in posterior mandible.

questions.question: A 'ground-glass' radiographic pattern of jaw bone is most characteristic of:
questions.option_b: Central giant cell granuloma
questions.explanation: Fibrous dysplasia produces a diffuse ground-glass appearance with bony expansion.

questions.question: A radiopaque mass fused to a tooth root with a thin radiolucent rim is most consistent with:
questions.option_b: Complex odontoma
questions.explanation: Cementoblastoma is a true neoplasm attached to the root with radiolucent halo.

questions.question: A child presents with multiple café-au-lait spots, polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, and endocrine abnormalities. This syndrome is:
questions.option_b: McCune–Albright syndrome
questions.explanation: McCune–Albright: polyostotic fibrous dysplasia + café-au-lait + endocrine hyperfunction.

questions.question: Which oral manifestation is typical of Peutz–Jeghers syndrome?
questions.option_b: Perioral and oral mucosal melanotic macules
questions.explanation: Peutz–Jeghers features mucocutaneous pigmentation (macules) and GI hamartomatous polyps.

questions.question: The most common cause of acute bacterial sialadenitis of the parotid is:
questions.option_b: Candida albicans
questions.explanation: Acute suppurative parotitis is commonly caused by S. aureus, often with dehydration/duct obstruction.

questions.question: Pain and swelling of the submandibular gland during meals most suggests:
questions.option_b: Pleomorphic adenoma
questions.explanation: Meal-time pain/swelling suggests obstructive sialolith in Wharton’s duct/submandibular gland.

questions.question: First-line imaging for suspected salivary duct stone in many cases is:
questions.option_b: Ultrasound (especially for submandibular/parotid)
questions.explanation: Ultrasound is a common first-line, noninvasive modality; CT can help if needed for small/complex stones.

questions.question: Adenoid cystic carcinoma is best characterized by:
questions.option_b: Perineural invasion and late distant metastasis
questions.explanation: ACC is malignant, infiltrative, perineural, and may metastasize late even after years.

questions.question: A benign palatal ulcer that mimics carcinoma clinically and histologically but heals spontaneously is:
questions.option_b: Erythroplakia
questions.explanation: Necrotizing sialometaplasia is self-limiting and often follows ischemia/trauma to minor salivary glands.

questions.question: Direct immunofluorescence in pemphigus vulgaris typically shows:
questions.option_a: Linear IgG at basement membrane zone
questions.option_b: Intercellular 'fishnet' IgG/C3 in epithelium
questions.explanation: Pemphigus shows intercellular deposition against desmogleins (fishnet pattern).

questions.question: Pemphigus vulgaris histology most typically shows:
questions.option_b: Suprabasal acantholysis with 'tombstone' basal cells
questions.explanation: PV produces suprabasal clefting due to acantholysis.

questions.question: Mucous membrane pemphigoid DIF pattern is classically:
questions.option_b: Linear IgG/C3 along basement membrane zone
questions.explanation: Pemphigoid targets hemidesmosomal/BMZ components, giving linear BMZ staining.

questions.question: Desquamative gingivitis is most commonly a clinical presentation of:
questions.option_b: Mucous membrane pemphigoid or erosive lichen planus
questions.explanation: Desquamative gingivitis is a descriptive term; common causes include MMP and erosive lichen planus.

questions.question: Which is the most important prognostic factor in oral SCC?
questions.option_b: Tumor stage including nodal status
questions.explanation: TNM stage, especially lymph node involvement, is a key determinant of prognosis.

questions.question: A red lesion that blanches on diascopy is most consistent with:
questions.option_b: Vascular lesion (e.g., hemangioma)
questions.explanation: Blanching indicates blood within vessels, supporting a vascular lesion.

questions.question: Actinic cheilitis is most strongly associated with:
questions.option_a: Chronic sun exposure of the lower lip and risk of SCC
questions.option_b: Allergic reaction to toothpaste
questions.explanation: Actinic cheilitis is a premalignant change of sun-exposed vermilion, especially lower lip.

questions.question: Which property best describes resistance to permanent deformation under compressive load?
questions.option_b: Compressive strength
questions.explanation: Compressive strength reflects resistance to failure under compressive stress.

questions.question: A high elastic modulus indicates a material is:
questions.option_b: More stiff
questions.explanation: Young’s modulus measures stiffness; higher modulus means less strain per stress.

questions.question: Conventional glass ionomer cement primarily sets by:
questions.option_b: Acid–base reaction between polyacid and fluoroaluminosilicate glass
questions.explanation: GIC sets via an acid–base reaction forming a cross-linked salt matrix.

questions.question: Which elastomeric impression material is relatively more hydrophilic and tolerates moisture better?
questions.option_b: Polyether
questions.explanation: Polyether is comparatively hydrophilic and shows good wettability in moist conditions.

questions.question: A key disadvantage of alginate impressions is:
questions.option_b: Poor dimensional stability due to syneresis/imbibition
questions.explanation: Alginate can lose/absorb water causing distortion; it should be poured promptly.

questions.question: Type IV dental stone is chosen primarily for:
questions.option_a: High setting expansion and low strength
questions.option_b: High strength and low setting expansion
questions.explanation: Type IV is a high-strength, low-expansion die stone for fixed prosthodontics.

questions.question: The primary purpose of silane coupling agent in resin composites is to:
questions.option_b: Bond inorganic filler particles to the resin matrix
questions.explanation: Silane chemically couples silica-based fillers to resin, improving strength and wear resistance.

questions.question: Compared with condensation silicone, addition silicone (PVS) is more dimensionally stable because it:
questions.option_b: Produces no volatile byproduct during setting
questions.explanation: PVS (addition silicone) does not release volatile byproducts, so shrinkage is minimal.

questions.question: Condensation silicone impressions may distort over time mainly due to:
questions.option_b: Alcohol byproduct evaporation
questions.explanation: Condensation silicones release alcohol; evaporation leads to dimensional change.

questions.question: Polyether impression materials are difficult to remove from pronounced undercuts because they are:
questions.option_b: Too brittle only
questions.explanation: Polyether has high stiffness, increasing risk of distortion or tearing in undercuts.

questions.question: Alginate should ideally be poured:
questions.option_a: Immediately or within a short time in humid conditions
questions.option_b: After 24 hours in open air
questions.explanation: Alginate undergoes syneresis/imbibition; prompt pouring minimizes dimensional change.

questions.question: The main setting reaction of zinc phosphate cement is:
questions.option_a: Acid–base reaction between phosphoric acid and zinc oxide
questions.option_b: Free-radical polymerization
questions.explanation: Zinc phosphate sets by an acid–base reaction producing a zinc phosphate matrix.

questions.question: Zinc phosphate cement is most likely to cause initial pulpal irritation because it:
questions.option_b: Releases eugenol
questions.explanation: The initial pH is low; deep preparations may need pulpal protection.

questions.question: Glass ionomer cement adheres to tooth structure primarily via:
questions.option_a: Ionic bonding to calcium in hydroxyapatite
questions.option_b: Only micromechanical retention
questions.explanation: Carboxylate groups chelate calcium, creating chemical adhesion.

questions.question: A key advantage of glass ionomer cement in caries-prone patients is:
questions.option_a: Highest fracture toughness of all cements
questions.option_b: Fluoride release and recharge
questions.option_c: Complete moisture insensitivity during early set
questions.explanation: Fluoride release/recharge can help reduce recurrent caries risk.

questions.question: Resin-modified glass ionomer (RMGIC) differs from conventional GIC because it:
questions.option_a: Sets only by polymerization with no acid–base reaction
questions.option_b: Combines acid–base reaction with resin polymerization
questions.explanation: RMGIC has dual setting (acid–base + light/chemical polymerization), improving early strength.

questions.question: Eugenol-containing temporary cements can interfere with resin bonding because eugenol:
questions.option_b: Scavenges free radicals and inhibits polymerization
questions.explanation: Eugenol inhibits free-radical polymerization of resin-based materials.

questions.question: In dental amalgam, creep is defined as:
questions.option_b: Time-dependent plastic deformation under stress
questions.explanation: Creep contributes to marginal breakdown under long-term occlusal forces.

questions.question: High-copper amalgams improved clinical performance primarily by reducing the:
questions.option_b: Gamma-2 phase (Sn7-8Hg)
questions.explanation: Elimination of the gamma-2 phase reduces corrosion and creep, improving strength.

questions.question: The oxygen-inhibited layer on light-cured resin is caused by:
questions.option_b: Oxygen quenching free radicals at the surface
questions.explanation: Oxygen inhibits polymerization at the surface, leaving a tacky unpolymerized layer.

questions.question: Polymerization shrinkage of resin composite is most clinically associated with:
questions.option_b: Marginal gap formation and microleakage
questions.option_d: Reduced stress in high C-factor cavities
questions.explanation: Shrinkage stress can debond margins, causing gaps, sensitivity, and recurrent caries.

questions.question: The configuration factor (C-factor) is the:
questions.option_b: Ratio of bonded to unbonded surfaces in a cavity
questions.explanation: Higher C-factor increases shrinkage stress due to constrained composite flow.

questions.question: Radiopacity in resin composites is mainly provided by:
questions.option_b: Radiopaque glass fillers containing barium/strontium/ytterbium
questions.explanation: Heavy-element fillers increase X-ray attenuation, improving radiographic visibility.

questions.question: Which ceramic is a silica-based glass ceramic commonly etched with HF and silanated for bonding?
questions.option_b: Lithium disilicate
questions.explanation: Lithium disilicate is HF-etchable and bonds well with silane + resin cement.

questions.question: Zirconia bonding is challenging because zirconia:
questions.option_b: Has little/no silica and is not effectively HF-etched
questions.explanation: Zirconia is polycrystalline; bonding relies on air abrasion and MDP chemistry rather than HF etching.

questions.question: A resin cement containing MDP is particularly useful for bonding to:
questions.option_b: Zirconia and base metals
questions.explanation: MDP phosphate monomer bonds to metal oxides on zirconia and certain alloys.

questions.question: The setting expansion of gypsum products is mainly due to:
questions.option_b: Crystal growth pressure during interlocking of gypsum crystals
questions.explanation: Crystallization and crystal impingement generate expansion.

questions.question: Increasing the water/powder ratio in gypsum generally results in:
questions.option_b: Lower strength and higher porosity
questions.explanation: More excess water leaves pores after setting, reducing strength.

questions.question: Vacuum mixing of gypsum primarily reduces:
questions.option_b: Air inclusion/porosity
questions.explanation: Vacuum mixing decreases air bubbles, improving density and strength.

questions.question: Impression material with the best tear strength among elastomers is typically:
questions.option_b: Polyether
questions.explanation: Polysulfide generally has high tear strength, useful in thin areas.

questions.question: A key disadvantage of polysulfide impression material is:
questions.option_b: Unpleasant odor/taste and longer setting time
questions.explanation: Polysulfides have longer setting times and characteristic odor; they can be messy.

questions.question: Impression compound is best described as:
questions.option_a: An elastomeric material used for crown impressions
questions.option_b: A thermoplastic impression material used mainly for preliminary impressions/border molding
questions.option_c: A hydrocolloid that sets by gelation
questions.explanation: Impression compound softens with heat and hardens on cooling; used for primary impressions and border molding.

questions.question: In casting, a properly designed sprue primarily serves to:
questions.option_b: Provide a channel and reservoir for molten metal to compensate for shrinkage
questions.explanation: Sprue delivers metal and acts as a reservoir to reduce shrinkage porosity.

questions.question: Shrinkage porosity in castings is most commonly caused by:
questions.option_b: Inadequate feeding during solidification
questions.explanation: If molten metal is not fed during solidification, localized shrinkage voids occur.

questions.question: Chromium improves corrosion resistance of stainless steel by:
questions.option_b: Forming a passive oxide layer (passivation)
questions.explanation: A chromium oxide layer protects the alloy from corrosion.

questions.question: Galvanic corrosion occurs when:
questions.option_a: Two dissimilar metals are electrically connected in an electrolyte (saliva)
questions.option_b: A single noble metal is present
questions.explanation: Dissimilar metals create an electrochemical cell in saliva, leading to corrosion.

questions.question: The coefficient of thermal expansion mismatch between tooth and restorative material can contribute to:
questions.option_b: Percolation/microleakage from thermal cycling
questions.explanation: Repeated expansion/contraction can open margins and increase leakage risk.

questions.question: Flow in dental waxes refers to:
questions.option_b: Time-dependent deformation under stress at a given temperature
questions.explanation: Wax flow increases with temperature and can distort patterns if not handled properly.

questions.question: Acrylic denture base resin polymerizes primarily by:
questions.option_b: Free-radical addition polymerization of MMA
questions.explanation: PMMA forms via free-radical polymerization of methyl methacrylate monomer.

questions.question: A major disadvantage of PMMA denture base resin is:
questions.option_a: Extremely high modulus with no flex
questions.option_b: Polymerization shrinkage and residual monomer irritation
questions.explanation: Residual monomer can irritate tissues; shrinkage can affect fit, especially if processing is poor.

questions.question: Which impression material is most dimensionally stable for delayed pouring and multiple pours?
questions.option_b: Addition silicone (PVS)
questions.explanation: PVS has excellent elastic recovery and dimensional stability for delayed pours.

questions.question: Which statement about alginate disinfection is most accurate?
questions.option_a: It cannot be disinfected at all
questions.option_b: Short immersion/spray disinfection can be used, but prolonged immersion may distort it
questions.explanation: Alginate can be disinfected, but prolonged immersion may cause imbibition and distortion.

questions.question: The primary reason to use a conditioner (polyacrylic acid) before placing GIC is to:
questions.option_a: Remove smear layer partially and improve chemical bonding
questions.option_b: Etch enamel deeply like phosphoric acid
questions.explanation: Conditioning removes smear layer and exposes calcium for better ionic bonding, without aggressive demineralization.

questions.question: In resin composites, increasing filler content generally:
questions.option_b: Decreases polymerization shrinkage and increases strength
questions.explanation: More filler reduces resin matrix volume, lowering shrinkage and improving mechanical properties.

questions.question: Water sorption in resin materials can cause:
questions.option_b: Plasticization, staining, and hydrolytic degradation
questions.explanation: Water can soften resin matrix and degrade interfaces, contributing to discoloration and reduced properties.

questions.question: The main clinical risk of a rough ceramic surface against enamel is:
questions.option_b: Increased wear of opposing enamel
questions.explanation: Rough hard ceramics can abrade opposing enamel; glazing/polishing reduces this.

questions.question: Which property best represents resistance to crack propagation in a brittle material?
questions.option_b: Fracture toughness
questions.explanation: Fracture toughness describes resistance to crack growth and catastrophic fracture.

questions.question: Toughness is best defined as:
questions.option_b: Energy absorbed before fracture (area under stress–strain curve)
questions.explanation: Toughness reflects a material’s ability to absorb energy before failure.

questions.question: Hardness primarily correlates with:
questions.option_b: Abrasion and resistance to indentation
questions.explanation: Hardness is a measure of resistance to scratching/indentation and relates to wear resistance.

questions.question: The main purpose of etching enamel with phosphoric acid is to:
questions.option_b: Create microporosities for micromechanical retention
questions.explanation: Acid etching increases surface energy and creates micro-roughness for resin tag formation.

questions.question: The coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatch between restoration and tooth primarily risks:
questions.option_b: Marginal leakage from thermal cycling
questions.explanation: CTE mismatch can stress margins during temperature changes, promoting microleakage.

questions.question: Solubility of luting cement is MOST critical for restorations with:
questions.option_b: Subgingival or poorly accessible margins
questions.explanation: Subgingival margins are harder to clean and more exposed to fluids; cement dissolution risks leakage and caries.

questions.question: Which impression material is most prone to permanent deformation if removed before complete set?
questions.option_b: Polyether
questions.explanation: Alginate has limited elastic recovery and must be removed after proper set to reduce distortion.

questions.question: The setting expansion of gypsum is primarily caused by:
questions.option_b: Crystal growth pressure as interlocking crystals form
questions.explanation: As gypsum crystals grow and impinge, they push each other and expand.

questions.question: An accelerator for gypsum products is:
questions.option_b: Potassium sulfate
questions.explanation: Potassium sulfate accelerates gypsum set; borax is a retarder.

questions.question: A retarder for gypsum products is:
questions.option_b: Sodium citrate
questions.explanation: Sodium citrate acts as a retarder by interfering with crystal growth.

questions.question: The primary role of the inhibitor (e.g., BHT) in resin composites is to:
questions.option_b: Prevent premature polymerization during storage
questions.explanation: Inhibitors prevent spontaneous curing in the bottle/syringe.

questions.question: Which light-curing parameter MOST affects depth of cure?
questions.option_b: Radiant exposure (irradiance × time) and increment thickness
questions.explanation: Depth of cure depends on energy delivered and thickness/opacity of the increment.

questions.question: Compared with microfilled composites, nanohybrid composites generally provide:
questions.option_b: Higher strength and good polishability balance
questions.explanation: Nanohybrids combine strength with acceptable polish retention.

questions.question: A common cause of porcelain chipping in PFM restorations is:
questions.option_b: Mismatch of metal and porcelain CTE or inadequate support/framework design
questions.explanation: CTE mismatch and poor support concentrate tensile stresses leading to chipping.

questions.question: High noble alloys are characterized by:
questions.option_b: No gold allowed
questions.explanation: High noble alloys have high noble content; they resist corrosion and tarnish.

questions.question: Nickel-containing alloys are clinically important because they:
questions.option_b: May cause allergic reactions in susceptible patients
questions.option_c: Are always more biocompatible than gold
questions.explanation: Nickel allergy is relatively common and relevant to dental alloys.

questions.question: The passive oxide layer essential for bonding to porcelain in base-metal alloys is mainly formed by:
questions.option_b: Chromium
questions.explanation: Chromium oxide provides corrosion resistance and supports porcelain bonding in many base-metal systems.

questions.question: Amalgam expansion risk increases with:
questions.option_b: Under-trituration and delayed condensation
questions.explanation: Under-trituration/delayed condensation can increase residual mercury and dimensional changes.

questions.question: Delayed expansion of amalgam is most associated with contamination by:
questions.option_b: EDTA
questions.explanation: Moisture reacts with zinc to produce hydrogen gas, causing delayed expansion.

questions.question: Polymerization shrinkage is greatest when:
questions.option_b: Monomer content is higher and filler content is lower
questions.explanation: More resin matrix (lower filler) leads to greater shrinkage.

questions.question: The main purpose of a dentin bonding agent is to:
questions.option_b: Form a hybrid layer and resin tags to micromechanically bond to dentin
questions.explanation: Adhesives infiltrate demineralized collagen to form a hybrid layer for bonding.

questions.question: Self-etch adhesives generally:
questions.option_a: Remove smear layer completely like phosphoric acid
questions.option_b: Incorporate smear layer into hybrid layer (modify rather than remove)
questions.explanation: Self-etch systems partially dissolve and integrate smear layer into the adhesive interface.

questions.question: For zirconia surface treatment prior to resin cementation, the MOST appropriate is:
questions.option_b: Air abrasion + MDP primer
questions.explanation: Zirconia requires micromechanical roughening (air abrasion) and chemical bonding via MDP.

questions.question: Silane is most effective for bonding to:
questions.option_b: Silica-based ceramics (glass ceramics)
questions.explanation: Silane couples resin to silica-containing glass ceramics after HF etching.

questions.question: Which property is most important for an elastomeric impression material to reproduce fine detail?
questions.option_b: Low contact angle (good wettability) and adequate flow before set
questions.explanation: Wettability and flow allow capture of fine surface details.

questions.question: Polyether impression material should be stored after setting:
questions.option_b: Dry for days without protection
questions.option_c: Briefly disinfected and stored in a sealed bag (avoid prolonged water immersion)
questions.explanation: Polyether can absorb water; avoid prolonged immersion to prevent distortion.

questions.question: The primary reason for using a tray adhesive is to:
questions.option_b: Prevent impression from separating from tray and distorting on removal
questions.explanation: Adhesive retains the impression in the tray to reduce distortion.

questions.question: The most accurate gypsum for removable die work is generally:
questions.option_b: Type III stone
questions.explanation: Type IV provides high strength and minimal expansion, ideal for dies.

questions.question: Hydrocolloid 'hysteresis' refers to:
questions.option_b: Difference between gelation and liquefaction temperatures
questions.explanation: Agar hydrocolloid liquefies and gels at different temperatures (hysteresis).

questions.question: A primary disadvantage of zinc phosphate cement is:
questions.option_b: High initial acidity and lack of chemical adhesion to tooth structure
questions.explanation: Zinc phosphate is strong but acidic initially and relies on mechanical retention.

questions.question: A major advantage of resin cements over conventional cements is:
questions.option_b: Higher bond strength to tooth/restoration with appropriate adhesive protocols
questions.explanation: Resin cements can bond and increase retention, but are technique sensitive.

questions.question: Polymerization of light-cured resin composites is initiated primarily by:
questions.option_b: Camphorquinone + amine (photoinitiator system)
questions.explanation: Camphorquinone absorbs blue light and, with amine, generates free radicals.

questions.question: The main reason for incremental curing of composite is to:
questions.option_b: Improve depth of cure and reduce shrinkage stress
questions.explanation: Smaller increments cure better and reduce shrinkage stress.

questions.question: Which property best represents resistance to crack propagation?
questions.option_b: Fracture toughness
questions.explanation: Fracture toughness measures resistance to crack growth, important for ceramics.

questions.question: A common method to reduce porosity in gypsum casts is:
questions.option_b: Vacuum mixing and proper vibration
questions.explanation: Vacuum mixing reduces air incorporation; vibration helps flow without trapping bubbles.

questions.question: Water/powder ratio increase generally results in gypsum casts that are:
questions.option_b: Weaker and more porous
questions.explanation: More water leaves more voids after set, reducing strength.

questions.question: An 'exothermic' setting reaction is most clinically relevant for:
questions.option_b: Acrylic resin polymerization (heat may damage tissues)
questions.explanation: PMMA polymerization is exothermic; heat can affect tissues during intraoral repairs.

questions.question: Residual monomer in acrylic resin can primarily cause:
questions.option_b: Mucosal irritation and reduced biocompatibility
questions.explanation: Unreacted monomer may irritate tissues; proper curing reduces residual monomer.

questions.question: The best disinfectant approach for PVS impressions is typically:
questions.option_b: Rinse, spray/immersion per manufacturer for recommended time, then rinse/dry
questions.explanation: Follow manufacturer protocols to disinfect without distorting the impression.

questions.question: Compared with ceramics, metals generally have:
questions.option_b: Higher ductility and toughness
questions.explanation: Metals are ductile and tough; ceramics are brittle with low tensile strength.

questions.question: The main function of filler particles in composites is to:
questions.option_b: Increase strength and reduce shrinkage and thermal expansion
questions.explanation: Fillers improve mechanical properties and reduce shrinkage/CTE.

questions.question: Which impression material exhibits the highest elastic recovery among common elastomers?
questions.option_b: Condensation silicone
questions.explanation: PVS shows excellent elastic recovery and dimensional stability.

questions.question: A key reason ceramics are susceptible to catastrophic failure is:
questions.option_b: Presence of flaws acting as stress concentrators and low tensile strength
questions.explanation: Ceramic flaws concentrate stress; brittle nature leads to crack propagation.

questions.question: For base-metal alloy casting, the investment should generally be:
questions.option_b: Phosphate-bonded
questions.explanation: Phosphate-bonded investments withstand higher casting temperatures of base-metal alloys.

questions.question: The main reason for using a die spacer is to:
questions.option_b: Create space for cement film thickness
questions.explanation: Die spacer provides controlled cement space to allow complete seating.

questions.question: A resin composite with higher filler loading typically shows:
questions.option_b: Higher wear resistance and lower shrinkage
questions.explanation: Higher filler reduces resin matrix volume, improving wear resistance and reducing shrinkage.

questions.question: A common disadvantage of glass ionomer cements is:
questions.option_b: Early moisture sensitivity during initial set
questions.explanation: GIC is sensitive to early moisture contamination and dehydration; protection is important.

questions.question: Compomers are best described as:
questions.option_b: Resin composites with polyacid-modified monomers that can have limited fluoride release
questions.explanation: Compomers are polyacid-modified resin composites with some fluoride release potential.

questions.question: The main cause of marginal breakdown in low-copper amalgam was:
questions.option_b: Gamma-2 phase with higher corrosion/creep
questions.explanation: Gamma-2 is weak and corrosion-prone, contributing to marginal breakdown; reduced in high-copper amalgams.

questions.question: The best definition of 'creep' is:
questions.option_b: Time-dependent plastic deformation under constant stress
questions.explanation: Creep is slow plastic deformation over time, relevant for amalgam under occlusal load.

questions.question: Which is the primary determinant of contact angle on a surface?
questions.option_b: Radiopacity
questions.explanation: Lower contact angle indicates better wettability and higher surface energy, improving impression/detail capture.

questions.question: To reduce polymerization shrinkage stress clinically, one effective strategy is:
questions.option_b: Use incremental layering and appropriate curing protocols
questions.explanation: Incremental layering and controlled curing reduce stress; eugenol can inhibit polymerization.

questions.question: The main purpose of a coupling agent in composites is to:
questions.option_b: Bond filler to resin matrix and transfer stress
questions.explanation: Silane coupling improves filler–matrix bonding and mechanical properties.

questions.question: Which statement about zinc polycarboxylate cement is correct?
questions.option_a: It has no adhesion to tooth
questions.option_b: It chemically bonds to tooth via chelation and is less irritating than zinc phosphate
questions.option_c: It is the strongest resin cement
questions.explanation: Polycarboxylate chelates calcium and is less acidic than zinc phosphate, offering some chemical adhesion.

questions.question: Which property describes the ability of a material to return to its original shape after removal of a deforming force?
questions.option_b: Elastic recovery
questions.explanation: Elastic recovery reflects how well a material returns to its original dimensions after deformation.

questions.question: Yield strength is best defined as:
questions.option_a: The stress at which a material fractures
questions.option_b: The stress at which plastic deformation begins
questions.explanation: Yield strength marks the onset of permanent (plastic) deformation.

questions.question: A higher Poisson’s ratio indicates:
questions.option_a: Greater longitudinal contraction with lateral expansion under tensile load
questions.option_b: Lower stiffness always
questions.explanation: Poisson’s ratio relates lateral strain to axial strain; higher values mean more lateral expansion for a given axial stretch.

questions.question: Which property is most relevant to brittleness?
questions.option_b: High ductility
questions.explanation: Brittle materials have low fracture toughness and fail with little plastic deformation.

questions.question: Resilience is best described as:
questions.option_b: Energy absorbed in the elastic region
questions.explanation: Resilience is the elastic energy storage capacity (area under elastic portion of stress–strain curve).

questions.question: A material with high damping capacity is useful in:
questions.option_b: Increasing radiopacity
questions.explanation: Damping reflects energy dissipation, helpful where vibration absorption is desired.

questions.question: In alginate, syneresis refers to:
questions.option_b: Loss of water leading to shrinkage
questions.explanation: Syneresis is exudation of liquid from gel, causing shrinkage.

questions.question: In alginate, imbibition refers to:
questions.option_b: Loss of water causing shrinkage
questions.explanation: Imbibition is water absorption by the gel, causing expansion.

questions.question: Which impression material has the best dimensional stability over time?
questions.option_b: Polysulfide
questions.explanation: PVS has excellent dimensional stability due to no volatile byproduct formation.

questions.question: The byproduct of condensation silicone setting reaction is:
questions.option_b: Alcohol
questions.explanation: Condensation silicones release alcohol, which can evaporate and cause shrinkage.

questions.question: Polyether impressions should be disinfected preferably by:
questions.option_b: Autoclaving
questions.explanation: Polyether can absorb water; follow manufacturer disinfection times to avoid distortion.

questions.question: The primary reason polysulfide is more difficult to remove from undercuts is:
questions.option_b: High stiffness after set
questions.explanation: Polysulfide has relatively low elastic recovery and can distort; many clinicians find it harder to remove due to material behavior and tray retention.

questions.question: Type III dental stone is typically used for:
questions.option_b: Preliminary casts for removable prostheses
questions.explanation: Type III is dental stone used for working casts (e.g., removable prosthodontics).

questions.question: Type V high-expansion die stone is used primarily to:
questions.option_b: Compensate for casting shrinkage of base-metal alloys
questions.explanation: Type V expands more to offset greater casting shrinkage of certain alloys.

questions.question: The setting reaction of gypsum is:
questions.option_b: Free-radical polymerization
questions.explanation: Calcium sulfate hemihydrate hydrates to dihydrate forming interlocking crystals.

questions.question: Increasing spatulation time of gypsum generally:
questions.option_a: Decreases setting time and increases setting expansion
questions.option_b: Increases setting time and decreases expansion
questions.explanation: More spatulation accelerates nucleation (faster set) and can increase expansion.

questions.question: Which additive acts as a gypsum retarder?
questions.option_b: Borax
questions.explanation: Borax is a common retarder; potassium sulfate is an accelerator.

questions.question: Glass ionomer cement adheres to tooth structure primarily by:
questions.option_b: Ionic bonding between carboxylates and calcium in hydroxyapatite
questions.explanation: GIC chemically bonds to tooth through ionic interaction with calcium.

questions.question: The main advantage of resin-modified glass ionomer over conventional GIC is:
questions.option_b: Improved early strength via resin polymerization
questions.explanation: RMGIC has dual cure, improving early properties versus conventional GIC.

questions.question: Zinc phosphate cement is primarily retained by:
questions.option_b: Micromechanical interlocking
questions.explanation: Zinc phosphate relies on mechanical retention; it does not chemically bond to tooth.

questions.question: Zinc polycarboxylate cement is notable because it:
questions.option_b: Can chemically bond to tooth and is less irritating than zinc phosphate
questions.option_d: Cannot be used as a luting agent
questions.explanation: Polycarboxylate chelates calcium and is relatively pulp-friendly.

questions.question: The main problem with eugenol under resin restorations is that eugenol:
questions.option_b: Inhibits free-radical polymerization
questions.explanation: Eugenol can scavenge radicals and compromise resin curing/bonding.

questions.question: Which cement provides the highest bond strength when used with appropriate adhesive systems?
questions.option_b: Conventional GIC
questions.explanation: Resin cements, with correct bonding, provide strong adhesion and low solubility.

questions.question: Polymerization shrinkage stress is minimized by:
questions.option_b: Incremental placement and controlled curing
questions.explanation: Incremental technique and appropriate curing reduce shrinkage stress and improve adaptation.

questions.question: The primary role of silane in composites is to:
questions.option_b: Couple filler particles to resin matrix
questions.explanation: Silane forms a bond between silica-based filler and resin matrix.

questions.question: Radiopacity in composites is increased mainly by:
questions.option_b: Radiopaque glass fillers (Ba/Sr/Zr)
questions.explanation: Heavy-element fillers increase radiopacity.

questions.question: Which factor most reduces oxygen-inhibited layer clinically?
questions.option_b: Using glycerin gel or mylar strip and curing
questions.explanation: Oxygen inhibits surface polymerization; barriers reduce oxygen contact during cure.

questions.question: Nanohybrid composites are designed to improve:
questions.option_b: Balance of strength and polish retention
questions.explanation: Nanohybrid filler distributions provide good mechanics and polishability.

questions.question: High-copper amalgams are superior because they reduce formation of:
questions.option_b: Gamma-1 (Ag-Hg) phase
questions.explanation: Reducing gamma-2 improves corrosion resistance and decreases creep.

questions.question: Delayed expansion of amalgam is most associated with:
questions.option_b: Moisture contamination in zinc-containing amalgam
questions.explanation: Moisture reacts with zinc producing hydrogen gas and delayed expansion.

questions.question: The primary reason to burnish amalgam margins is to:
questions.option_b: Improve marginal adaptation and reduce microleakage risk
questions.explanation: Burnishing improves marginal seal and surface smoothness.

questions.question: Glass ceramics (e.g., lithium disilicate) are best bonded by:
questions.option_b: HF etch + silane + resin cement
questions.explanation: Silica-based ceramics respond to HF etching and silanization for resin bonding.

questions.question: Zirconia is best bonded by:
questions.option_b: Air abrasion + MDP primer/cement
questions.explanation: Zirconia requires micromechanical roughening plus phosphate monomer (MDP) chemistry.

questions.question: A major cause of enamel wear against ceramics is:
questions.option_b: Surface roughness/glaze loss on a hard ceramic
questions.explanation: Hard, rough ceramic surfaces can abrade opposing enamel; polishing/glazing reduces wear.

questions.question: Fracture toughness is especially important for:
questions.option_b: Gypsum
questions.explanation: Ceramics are brittle; higher fracture toughness reduces crack propagation.

questions.question: Stainless steel corrosion resistance depends on:
questions.option_b: Chromium oxide passive film
questions.explanation: Chromium forms a passive oxide layer preventing corrosion.

questions.question: Phosphate-bonded investments are preferred for:
questions.option_b: Base-metal alloys requiring high casting temperatures
questions.explanation: Base-metal alloys need high-temperature investments such as phosphate-bonded.

questions.question: The main purpose of a sprue and reservoir in casting is to:
questions.option_b: Provide metal flow and feed solidification shrinkage
questions.explanation: Reservoir compensates shrinkage and reduces shrink-porosity.

questions.question: A die spacer is used primarily to:
questions.option_b: Create space for cement film thickness
questions.explanation: Die spacer provides controlled cement space for seating.

questions.question: Which property is most relevant to tear resistance in impression materials?
questions.option_b: Tear strength
questions.explanation: Tear strength reflects resistance to tearing during removal from undercuts.

questions.question: Hydrophilicity is most advantageous in impression materials because it:
questions.option_b: Improves wetting and detail capture in moist environments
questions.explanation: Better wettability improves capture of fine details when moisture is present.

questions.question: The primary function of tray adhesive is to:
questions.option_b: Prevent separation of impression from tray and distortion
questions.explanation: It secures the impression to the tray, reducing distortion.

questions.question: Water sorption in resin composites most commonly leads to:
questions.option_b: Plasticization and staining over time
questions.explanation: Water can soften the resin matrix and contribute to discoloration and degradation.

questions.question: The degree of conversion in composites is improved by:
questions.option_b: Adequate radiant exposure and appropriate increment thickness
questions.explanation: Adequate energy delivery and thin increments improve conversion.

questions.question: The primary purpose of dentin conditioning before conventional GIC placement is to:
questions.option_a: Remove smear layer and improve chemical bonding
questions.option_b: Increase enamel etch patterns
questions.explanation: Polyacrylic acid conditioner removes smear layer and improves GIC bonding.

questions.question: A common reason for porosity in acrylic denture base resin is:
questions.option_b: Monomer boiling from high exotherm/processing errors
questions.explanation: Processing heat can cause monomer vaporization and porosity.

questions.question: Residual monomer in acrylic resin is reduced most by:
questions.option_b: Proper polymerization/heat-curing cycle and post-polymerization
questions.explanation: Appropriate curing reduces residual monomer and improves biocompatibility.

questions.question: Biocompatibility concerns with nickel alloys are mainly related to:
questions.option_b: Allergic hypersensitivity reactions
questions.explanation: Nickel allergy is a key clinical concern.

questions.question: A key disadvantage of zinc phosphate cement is:
questions.option_b: High initial acidity and lack of chemical adhesion
questions.explanation: Zinc phosphate is strong but initially acidic and non-adhesive.

questions.question: RMGIC differs from composite resin because RMGIC:
questions.option_b: Can release fluoride and has acid–base component
questions.explanation: RMGIC combines resin polymerization with acid–base reaction and fluoride release.

questions.question: The main reason to avoid prolonged storage of alginate impressions is:
questions.option_b: Dimensional change from syneresis/imbibition
questions.explanation: Alginate changes dimension due to water loss or gain.

questions.question: Which ceramic is most translucent among common options?
questions.option_b: Feldspathic porcelain
questions.explanation: Feldspathic porcelain is highly translucent, though mechanically weaker than zirconia.

questions.question: Transformation toughening is most associated with:
questions.option_b: Zirconia
questions.explanation: Zirconia undergoes stress-induced phase transformation that increases toughness.

questions.question: A major advantage of polyether is:
questions.option_b: High hydrophilicity and detail reproduction
questions.explanation: Polyether is relatively hydrophilic and accurate (though stiffer after set).

questions.question: A disadvantage of polyether is:
questions.option_b: It is stiff and can be difficult to remove from undercuts
questions.explanation: High stiffness can make removal difficult and risk tearing/distortion.

questions.question: Which curing light issue most risks undercuring?
questions.option_b: Low irradiance, short exposure, thick increments
questions.explanation: Underdelivery of radiant exposure reduces depth of cure and conversion.

questions.question: The smear layer is best described as:
questions.option_b: A debris layer produced by instrumentation that occludes dentinal tubules
questions.explanation: Smear layer is a debris layer on dentin that can affect bonding and sensitivity.

questions.question: A common cause of porcelain-metal debonding is:
questions.option_b: Inadequate or excessive oxide layer and contamination
questions.explanation: Porcelain bonds to a controlled oxide layer; incorrect oxide or contamination reduces bond.

questions.question: The main purpose of glazing porcelain is to:
questions.option_b: Create a smooth surface, reduce plaque and wear on opposing enamel
questions.explanation: Glazing improves surface smoothness and reduces abrasion and plaque retention.

questions.question: Which luting agent has the highest film thickness if mixed improperly?
questions.option_b: Resin cement
questions.explanation: Zinc phosphate can have higher film thickness if mixed too thick; technique affects all cements.

questions.question: The best way to reduce water-related distortion of hydrocolloids is to:
questions.option_a: Store in dry air for days
questions.option_b: Pour immediately or store briefly in 100% humidity
questions.explanation: Brief storage in high humidity and prompt pouring minimizes dimensional change.

questions.question: Creep is most clinically relevant to:
questions.option_b: Amalgam marginal breakdown
questions.explanation: Amalgam creep contributes to marginal breakdown over time.

questions.question: Sorption and solubility of resin-based materials are tested per standards in:
questions.option_b: Dry vacuum only
questions.explanation: Water sorption/solubility are evaluated after storage in water for defined periods.

questions.question: A key benefit of higher filler loading in composites is:
questions.option_b: Less shrinkage and better wear resistance
questions.explanation: Higher filler reduces resin matrix, improving wear and decreasing shrinkage.

questions.question: The coupling agent in glass ceramics bonding protocol is:
questions.option_b: Silane
questions.explanation: Silane couples resin to silica-based ceramics after HF etching.

questions.question: The most important property for a temporary crown material to withstand occlusal forces is:
questions.option_b: Flexural strength
questions.explanation: Flexural strength is critical for resisting functional bending forces.

questions.question: A principal disadvantage of ZOE impression paste is:
questions.option_b: It is rigid and cannot be used in deep undercuts
questions.explanation: ZOE paste sets rigidly and is unsuitable for undercuts.

questions.question: The main difference between autopolymerizing and heat-cured PMMA is that autopolymerizing PMMA:
questions.option_b: Typically has higher residual monomer and lower properties
questions.explanation: Cold-cure resins often have higher residual monomer and lower mechanical properties.

questions.question: The primary cause of 'voids' in elastomeric impressions is:
questions.option_b: Inadequate mixing and air incorporation, poor syringing technique
questions.explanation: Air entrapment during mixing/syringing causes voids and defects.

questions.question: A common reason for marginal breakdown of low-copper amalgam is:
questions.option_b: Gamma-2 phase corrosion and creep
questions.explanation: Gamma-2 phase in low-copper amalgam is weak and corrosion-prone, contributing to marginal breakdown.

questions.question: The main effect of increasing filler particle size in composites (without changing load) is generally:
questions.option_b: Rougher surface finish and poorer polish retention
questions.explanation: Larger fillers can protrude and be plucked, worsening surface smoothness and polish retention.

questions.question: In casting, 'back pressure porosity' is primarily related to:
questions.option_b: Inadequate venting and trapped gases opposing metal flow
questions.explanation: Trapped gases in the mold can prevent complete filling and cause porosity.

questions.question: The most appropriate surface treatment for bonding to metal (non-precious) with resin cement is typically:
questions.option_b: Air abrasion/roughening + metal primer (e.g., MDP-based)
questions.explanation: Micromechanical roughening plus chemical primer improves resin bonding to many metals.

questions.question: In the ICCMS approach, an initial caries lesion limited to enamel is best managed by:
questions.option_a: Immediate operative restoration in all cases
questions.option_b: Non-operative preventive care and monitoring
questions.explanation: Non-cavitated enamel lesions are typically managed with risk control, fluoride, sealing/infiltration, and monitoring.

questions.question: Which is the primary purpose of rubber dam isolation during operative procedures?
questions.option_b: Improve moisture control and enhance adhesive performance
questions.explanation: Rubber dam improves moisture control, visibility, and safety, which is critical for adhesive dentistry.

questions.question: For a proximal Class II composite restoration, the main function of the wedge is to:
questions.option_b: Prevent oxygen inhibition
questions.option_c: Adapt the matrix to the gingival margin and slightly separate teeth
questions.explanation: Wedges adapt the matrix at the gingival margin and provide slight tooth separation to compensate for matrix thickness.

questions.question: Which matrix system is generally preferred for posterior Class II composite restorations to achieve proper contact?
questions.option_b: Sectional matrix with separation ring
questions.explanation: Sectional matrices with separation rings are associated with better proximal contacts for posterior composite.

questions.question: The C-factor of a cavity is the:
questions.option_b: Ratio of bonded to unbonded surfaces
questions.explanation: Higher C-factor increases polymerization shrinkage stress because more surfaces are bonded and constrain flow.

questions.question: Which incremental placement technique is most effective in reducing shrinkage stress in a Class I composite?
questions.option_b: Oblique incremental layering
questions.option_c: Pack in one increment then polish
questions.option_d: Cure only from the buccal surface
questions.explanation: Oblique increments reduce the number of bonded walls per increment, lowering shrinkage stress.

questions.question: Over-drying etched dentin (etch-and-rinse) primarily causes:
questions.option_b: Collapse of collagen and reduced resin infiltration
questions.explanation: Excessive air drying collapses the exposed collagen network, compromising hybrid layer formation.

questions.question: Selective enamel etching refers to:
questions.option_b: Etching enamel with phosphoric acid and using self-etch for dentin
questions.explanation: Selective enamel etch improves enamel bonding while limiting dentin over-etching when using self-etch adhesives.

questions.question: The smear layer is best described as:
questions.option_a: A protective mineral layer that enhances bonding
questions.option_b: A debris layer that occludes dentinal tubules and can interfere with bonding
questions.explanation: The smear layer is debris produced by cutting that partially blocks tubules; bonding can remove or modify it.

questions.question: A liner is most appropriately indicated when:
questions.option_a: Preparation is shallow in enamel only
questions.option_b: Deep dentin is close to the pulp and needs protection
questions.explanation: Deep preparations may need a liner/base to protect the pulp and provide a seal.

questions.question: For a small mechanical pulp exposure in a vital tooth when direct pulp capping is indicated, the preferred material is:
questions.option_b: Calcium silicate (e.g., MTA/Biodentine)
questions.explanation: Calcium silicate materials provide biocompatibility and sealing and promote dentin bridge formation.

questions.question: The primary goal of finishing and polishing a composite restoration is to:
questions.option_b: Improve smoothness, reduce plaque retention, and enhance esthetics
questions.explanation: A smooth surface reduces staining and plaque retention and improves longevity and gingival health.

questions.question: Immediate management of postoperative sensitivity due to a high occlusal contact on a new restoration is:
questions.option_b: Occlusal adjustment and reassessment
questions.explanation: High spots can cause sensitivity and pain; adjusting occlusion is a first-line step.

questions.question: Which factor most increases polymerization shrinkage stress?
questions.option_b: Higher C-factor
questions.explanation: High C-factor cavities constrain shrinkage, increasing stress at the bonded interface.

questions.question: In deep caries management, selective removal to firm/leathery dentin is performed to:
questions.option_b: Avoid pulp exposure while allowing sealing of the lesion
questions.explanation: Selective removal reduces exposure risk; sealing reduces bacterial activity and can arrest the lesion.

questions.question: For adhesive restoration of a subgingival Class V lesion with bleeding, the preferred approach is:
questions.option_b: Rubber dam plus retraction/hemostasis
questions.option_d: Use only a matrix without isolation
questions.explanation: Blood/saliva contamination compromises bonding; isolation and hemostasis are essential.

questions.question: A key indication for using GIC/RMGIC in operative dentistry is:
questions.option_a: Maximum wear resistance on occlusal surfaces
questions.option_b: Chemical adhesion and fluoride release in high-caries-risk situations
questions.explanation: GIC/RMGIC can bond chemically and release fluoride, useful in appropriate high-risk cases.

questions.question: Black’s Class II involves caries on:
questions.option_b: Proximal surfaces of posterior teeth
questions.option_c: Proximal surfaces of anterior teeth not involving incisal edge
questions.explanation: Class II lesions affect proximal surfaces of premolars/molars.

questions.question: Black’s Class V lesions are located on:
questions.option_b: Occlusal pits only
questions.option_c: Cervical third of facial or lingual surfaces
questions.explanation: Class V lesions are in the cervical third on facial/lingual surfaces.

questions.question: Soft-start/ramped curing is intended to:
questions.option_b: Reduce shrinkage stress by slowing early polymerization and allowing flow
questions.explanation: Lower initial irradiance can allow more flow before gel point, reducing stress.

questions.question: Beveling enamel margins in an anterior composite primarily helps by:
questions.option_b: Increasing enamel bonding area and improving esthetic blend
questions.explanation: Bevel increases surface area and creates a gradual transition that improves esthetics and seal.

questions.question: The most technique-sensitive aspect of posterior composite compared with amalgam is:
questions.option_b: Moisture control and proper adhesive protocol
questions.option_d: Need for rubber dam in all cases by law
questions.explanation: Adhesive bonding requires strict moisture control and correct multi-step technique.

questions.question: Flowable composite is best described as:
questions.option_a: Higher filler and higher wear resistance than packable
questions.option_b: Better adaptation to irregularities but generally lower wear resistance
questions.option_d: Always used for occlusal load-bearing surfaces
questions.explanation: Flowables adapt well but have lower filler; use selectively as liners/small restorations.

questions.question: The 'open sandwich' technique refers to:
questions.option_b: GIC/RMGIC placed cervically and left exposed at gingival margin
questions.option_c: Amalgam under composite in all cases
questions.explanation: An open sandwich leaves GIC exposed at cervical margin, useful when margin is on dentin/cementum.

questions.question: A common cause of weak proximal contact in a Class II composite is:
questions.option_b: Improper matrix contour and inadequate tooth separation
questions.explanation: Matrix thickness requires separation; without it, contacts are often open or weak.

questions.question: Caries detector dye is best used to:
questions.option_b: Assist differentiation of infected vs affected dentin (with limitations)
questions.explanation: Dyes may over-stain affected dentin; they are an adjunct to tactile/visual cues.

questions.question: Affected dentin is:
questions.option_a: Irreversibly infected and must be removed
questions.option_b: Demineralized but potentially remineralizable dentin that may be preserved under a seal
questions.option_c: Sound dentin with smear layer only
questions.explanation: Affected dentin has altered mineral content but can be preserved if sealed; infected dentin is soft and heavily contaminated.

questions.question: Which symptom pattern best suggests irreversible pulpitis?
questions.option_a: Brief cold sensitivity that resolves immediately
questions.option_b: Spontaneous pain with lingering thermal response
questions.option_d: Pain only on biting with no thermal issues
questions.explanation: Lingering thermal pain and spontaneous episodes are typical of irreversible pulpitis.

questions.question: For traditional amalgam cavity prep, primary retention is achieved by:
questions.option_b: Occlusal dovetail and convergent walls
questions.explanation: Amalgam relies on mechanical retention: wall convergence and occlusal dovetail design.

questions.question: When excavating deep caries near the pulp, the preferred instrument is often:
questions.option_b: Low-speed round bur or spoon excavator with tactile control
questions.explanation: Controlled excavation near pulp reduces risk of iatrogenic exposure.

questions.question: A proximal overhang after restoration placement should be managed by:
questions.option_b: Remove and recontour with finishing strips/burs
questions.explanation: Overhangs promote plaque retention and periodontal inflammation; they should be corrected.

questions.question: A 'white line' at a composite margin most commonly indicates:
questions.option_b: Marginal fracture/debonding at enamel margin
questions.explanation: A white line can be a sign of marginal gap or enamel microfracture at the margin.

questions.question: Best surface treatment for composite repair is typically:
questions.option_a: Add new composite directly without prep
questions.option_b: Roughen/air-abrade, clean, apply adhesive, then add composite
questions.explanation: Repair requires micromechanical roughness plus chemical bonding via adhesive.

questions.question: Stepwise excavation is intended to:
questions.option_b: Allow sealing, reduce lesion activity, and promote tertiary dentin before re-entry
questions.explanation: Sealing reduces bacteria; pulpal response may form tertiary dentin, reducing exposure risk at re-entry.

questions.question: Bulk-fill composites are designed to:
questions.option_a: Be placed only in 1 mm increments
questions.option_b: Allow increased depth of cure within manufacturer limits with controlled shrinkage stress
questions.option_c: Eliminate the need for curing light
questions.explanation: Bulk-fill materials allow thicker increments (often 4–5 mm) if light and protocol are adequate.

questions.question: Resin infiltration (e.g., ICON) is primarily indicated for:
questions.option_b: Non-cavitated proximal enamel lesions
questions.explanation: Infiltration can arrest non-cavitated enamel lesions by occluding porosities.

questions.question: A preventive resin restoration (PRR) is best suited for:
questions.option_b: Small pit-and-fissure lesion with minimal enamel breakdown
questions.explanation: PRR conserves tooth structure and seals fissures while restoring minimal caries.

questions.question: Radiographic proximal lesion limited to outer enamel in a low-risk patient is generally managed by:
questions.option_b: Non-operative management (fluoride, diet control) and monitoring
questions.explanation: Early enamel lesions can often be arrested non-operatively, especially in low-risk patients.

questions.question: A common reason for open gingival margins in Class II composite restorations is:
questions.option_b: Poor matrix adaptation at gingival seat and inadequate wedging
questions.explanation: Inadequate gingival seal produces voids and open margins.

questions.question: Polymerization shrinkage can cause failure by:
questions.option_b: Creating stresses that debond margins if adhesion is inadequate
questions.explanation: Shrinkage stress can exceed bond strength, creating marginal gaps and microleakage.

questions.question: A major factor that reduces degree of conversion in deep composite is:
questions.option_b: Excessive increment thickness beyond depth of cure
questions.explanation: Thick increments reduce light penetration and conversion, leading to weak restoration and sensitivity.

questions.question: Which contamination most compromises bonding if it occurs after etching and before adhesive placement?
questions.option_b: Saliva/blood contamination
questions.explanation: Saliva/blood contamination reduces surface energy and inhibits resin infiltration; requires cleaning/re-etching per protocol.

questions.question: Best management for salivary contamination of etched enamel is to:
questions.option_b: Rinse, dry, re-etch enamel briefly, then continue
questions.explanation: Re-etching restores the etched microstructure for reliable bonding.

questions.question: When using calcium hydroxide as a liner in a very deep area, it is generally recommended to:
questions.option_a: Leave it exposed and place composite directly
questions.option_b: Cover it with RMGIC/base before final restoration
questions.explanation: Ca(OH)2 is weak/soluble; covering with RMGIC improves seal and support.

questions.question: The primary reason to use a wedge in a Class II restoration besides matrix adaptation is:
questions.option_b: Provide slight tooth separation to compensate for matrix thickness
questions.explanation: Separation helps achieve tight proximal contact after matrix removal.

questions.question: A major advantage of sectional matrix systems over circumferential matrices for composites is:
questions.option_b: Improved proximal contact and emergence profile
questions.explanation: Sectional matrices better reproduce anatomic contour and contact.

questions.question: If a composite restoration shows marginal staining without softness on probing and no radiographic caries, the best management is often:
questions.option_b: Polish/refurbish and monitor, addressing risk factors
questions.explanation: Not all marginal staining indicates secondary caries; refurbish and monitor when appropriate.

questions.question: The most important determinant of whether to restore a proximal lesion detected radiographically is:
questions.option_b: Presence of cavitation/activity and caries risk assessment
questions.explanation: Caries management is risk- and activity-based; cavitation often necessitates operative care.

questions.question: In minimally invasive dentistry, the purpose of sealing fissures over non-cavitated lesions is to:
questions.option_b: Cut off nutrient supply to bacteria and arrest lesion
questions.explanation: A good seal can arrest lesion progression by limiting biofilm access and diffusion.

questions.question: In a Class II composite, which step most directly contributes to a proper tight contact?
questions.option_b: Using separation ring and properly contoured sectional matrix
questions.explanation: Tooth separation with an appropriate matrix contour is crucial for contact formation.

questions.question: The most common reason for postoperative sensitivity after a Class II composite is:
questions.option_b: Microleakage or high occlusion
questions.explanation: Sensitivity is frequently due to microleakage from bonding issues or premature contacts/high occlusion.

questions.question: A 'white line' at a composite margin most often indicates:
questions.option_b: Marginal fracture/debonding of enamel margin
questions.explanation: A white line may reflect enamel microfracture or marginal gap at the interface.

questions.question: In minimally invasive dentistry, the primary objective is to:
questions.option_b: Preserve tooth structure while controlling disease activity
questions.explanation: MID focuses on risk-based prevention and conservative operative care.

questions.question: ICCMS caries staging uses which key inputs?
questions.option_b: Lesion severity and activity assessment plus patient risk
questions.explanation: ICCMS combines lesion staging, activity, and patient-level risk to guide management.

questions.question: A cavitated proximal lesion radiographically in dentin should be managed by:
questions.option_b: Operative restoration because cavitation prevents reliable biofilm control
questions.explanation: Cavitated lesions are difficult to clean; operative management is usually required.

questions.question: The most important factor for successful adhesive bonding is:
questions.option_b: Moisture control and correct adhesive protocol
questions.explanation: Contamination and protocol errors are major causes of bond failure.

questions.question: Selective caries removal is MOST appropriate when:
questions.option_a: Lesion is shallow in enamel only
questions.option_b: Deep lesion near pulp to avoid exposure while achieving a good seal
questions.explanation: Selective removal preserves pulpal vitality in deep lesions while sealing reduces bacterial activity.

questions.question: In stepwise excavation, the best reason to re-enter after months is:
questions.option_b: To reduce risk of exposure by allowing lesion arrest and tertiary dentin formation
questions.explanation: Sealing decreases bacteria; pulpal defense increases dentin barrier before re-entry.

questions.question: When using a sectional matrix for Class II, the separation ring primarily helps to:
questions.option_b: Compensate for matrix thickness and produce a tight proximal contact
questions.explanation: Separation provides contact tightness after band removal.

questions.question: A wedge is most important in Class II composites to:
questions.option_b: Prevent oxygen inhibition
questions.option_c: Adapt matrix at gingival margin and prevent overhangs/gingival gaps
questions.explanation: Wedge seals gingival margin and improves matrix adaptation to prevent voids/overhangs.

questions.question: The best management of salivary contamination on etched enamel is:
questions.option_b: Rinse/dry and re-etch enamel briefly, then continue bonding
questions.explanation: Re-etch restores enamel surface energy and microroughness for bonding.

questions.question: The most common cause of open proximal contact in Class II composite is:
questions.option_b: Insufficient tooth separation and poor matrix contour/adaptation
questions.explanation: Without separation and proper contour, matrix thickness yields weak contact.

questions.question: A cervical (Class V) lesion on root surface in a high caries-risk patient is often best restored with:
questions.option_b: Glass ionomer/RMGIC due to chemical adhesion and fluoride release
questions.explanation: GIC/RMGIC is moisture tolerant relative to resin and releases fluoride, helpful on root surfaces.

questions.question: The 'open sandwich' technique means:
questions.option_b: RMGIC base left exposed at gingival margin while composite covers occlusal portion
questions.explanation: Open sandwich leaves GIC exposed cervically to improve seal on dentin/cementum margins.

questions.question: The main risk of over-drying etched dentin in etch-and-rinse systems is:
questions.option_b: Collapse of collagen network reducing infiltration and bond strength
questions.explanation: Collapsed collagen limits hybrid layer formation, causing weaker bonds and sensitivity.

questions.question: In self-etch adhesives, enamel bonding is often improved by:
questions.option_b: Selective enamel etching with phosphoric acid
questions.explanation: Selective enamel etch enhances micromechanical retention on enamel.

questions.question: C-factor is highest in which cavity type?
questions.option_b: Class I
questions.explanation: Class I has many bonded surfaces relative to free surface, yielding high C-factor.

questions.question: Which technique reduces shrinkage stress most effectively?
questions.option_b: Incremental layering and controlled curing
questions.explanation: Incremental placement reduces stress and improves depth of cure.

questions.question: A common cause of marginal ditching in composite is:
questions.option_b: Inadequate curing and/or aggressive finishing at margins
questions.explanation: Under-cured resin and aggressive finishing can weaken margins leading to ditching.

questions.question: For direct pulp capping (when indicated), the preferred material is:
questions.option_b: MTA/Biodentine (calcium silicate)
questions.explanation: Calcium silicate materials provide bioactivity and sealing for pulp capping.

questions.question: In deep cavities, calcium hydroxide liners should generally be:
questions.option_b: Covered by RMGIC/base to protect and seal
questions.explanation: Ca(OH)2 is weak/soluble; covering improves seal and support.

questions.question: The best endpoint for caries removal in a deep lesion on the pulpal wall is often:
